explanation.title: Structuring Text with Headings, Paragraphs, and Inline Tags
explanation.content:
# Headings and Paragraphs Text is the foundation of almost every webpage. HTML provides specific elements for every kind of text, from page titles down to individual important words. Choosing the right element is important for accessibility, search engine optimisation, and visual clarity. ## The Six Heading Levels HTML has six levels of headings, `

` through `

`: ```html

Main Page Title

Section Heading

Subsection

Sub-subsection

Minor heading
Smallest heading
``` Key rules: - Use only **one `

`** per page — it tells search engines and screen readers what the page is about - Headings must be used in **logical order** — never skip from h1 to h4 - Choose heading levels based on **document structure**, not visual size ## Paragraphs ```html

A paragraph groups related sentences into a block of text.

Each paragraph element creates vertical spacing above and below.

``` Browsers collapse all whitespace inside HTML to a single space. To force a line break within a paragraph, use `
`: ```html

Line one.
Line two on the same paragraph.

``` ## Inline Text Elements These elements style or label individual words or phrases inside a paragraph: | Element | Default appearance | Semantic meaning | |---|---|---| | `` | **Bold** | Importance | | `` | *Italic* | Emphasis | | `` | Underline | Stylistic | | `` | Highlighted | Highlighted | | `` | Smaller | Fine print | | `` | ~~Strikethrough~~ | Deleted text | | `` | Subscript | H₂O | | `` | Superscript | x² | | `` | `Monospace` | Code snippet | ## Horizontal Rule and Line Break ```html

``` Both `
` and `
` are **void elements** — they have no content and no closing tag. In the code example below, you will see all six heading levels and several inline text elements used in a realistic article layout.

codeExample.explanation:
- `

` through `

` — three heading levels establish the content hierarchy for search engines and screen readers - `powerful` — bold text that also signals importance to assistive technology - `beginner-friendly` — italic text that signals stress emphasis - `artificial intelligence` — highlights text with a yellow background by default - `
` — a horizontal line representing a thematic break; a void element with no closing tag - `print()` — renders text in a monospace font to indicate computer code

exercise.title: Write a Formatted Bio Article
exercise.instructions:
Create a bio page for a fictional person. Use: one h1 for the person's full name, one h2 for 'About', a paragraph with at least one strong and one em tag, one h2 for 'Skills', a paragraph with at least one code tag, one hr between sections, and one small tag for a footer note. The page must have complete HTML5 structure.

Expected: page with h1, two h2 elements, hr, and inline text elements all used correctly.
exercise.starterCode:
About

Skills
exercise.solutionCode:
Priya Verma

About

Priya is a full-stack developer with three years of experience.

Skills

She writes Python and JavaScript every day.

Last updated: January 2025
hints:
💡 Place the h1 directly after the opening body tag with the person's name:

Full Name

💡 Inline tags go inside a p tag:

She is a developer with experience.
💡 The hr tag is a void element — write it as <hr> with no closing tag and no content.

questions.question: How many h1 elements should a well-structured HTML page have?
options: As many as needed — one per section
Exactly one — it represents the main topic of the page
None — h1 is deprecated in HTML5
questions.explanation: A well-structured HTML page should have exactly one h1 element representing the page's main topic. Search engines use the h1 to understand what the page is about, and screen readers use it as a navigation landmark. Using multiple h1 elements dilutes the page's focus for both users and search engines.

questions.question: What is the semantic difference between strong and b in HTML?
options: There is no difference — they render identically
strong signals importance to browsers and assistive technology; b is purely visual bold
b is stronger emphasis than strong
strong only works inside headings; b works everywhere
questions.explanation: Both strong and b render text in bold by default, but they carry different semantic meanings. strong signals that the text is of serious importance — screen readers may announce it with extra emphasis. b is purely presentational bold with no extra meaning. HTML5 recommends using strong for important text and b only when no semantic meaning is intended.

questions.question: Which element creates a line break without starting a new paragraph?
questions.explanation:
The br element (break) inserts a single line break within the current paragraph or block without creating a new paragraph element. It is a void element — written as
with no closing tag. Use it sparingly; for separating unrelated content, a new p element is more semantically appropriate.
